questions.explanationMd: Average Precision rewards retrieving relevant documents early. If a relevant document is **never retrieved**, it contributes nothing—effectively a precision contribution of **0**—which lowers AP and therefore MAP.

questions.questionMd: Which formula correctly defines the F1 score in terms of Precision (`P`) and Recall (`R`)?

options.text: 2PR / (P + R)
R / (P + R)

questions.explanationMd: The F1 score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall: `2PR / (P + R)`. It rewards systems that balance the two measures rather than maximizing only one.

questions.questionMd: Recall measures which aspect of retrieval performance?

options.text: The average precision at each recall level across multiple queries
The fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant
The fraction of all relevant documents that were successfully retrieved
The rank position of the first relevant document in the result list

questions.explanationMd: Recall asks: of **all relevant documents that exist**, how many did the system retrieve? That makes it especially important in tasks where missing relevant items is costly.

questions.questionMd: A search application requires that Recall be weighted more heavily than Precision. Which setting of `β` in the `Fβ` formula achieves this?

options.text: β = 1, because this balances Precision and Recall and therefore emphasizes neither
β = 0, because this setting removes Precision from the calculation entirely
β > 1, because larger β values increase the weight assigned to Recall in the harmonic mean
β < 1, because smaller β values shift focus away from Precision toward Recall

questions.explanationMd: In `Fβ`, values of **β > 1** weight Recall more heavily, while **β < 1** emphasizes Precision. `β = 1` gives the standard balanced F1 score.

questions.questionMd: Under the TREC standard four-point graded relevance scale, which label is assigned to a document that is a perfect, exact answer to the query?

options.text: 4 — Authoritative source
2 — Highly relevant
3 — Perfectly relevant
1 — Partially relevant

questions.explanationMd: In the four-point graded scheme described in the course, **3** corresponds to a **perfectly relevant** document: an exact or ideal answer to the information need.

questions.questionMd: Why is Normalized DCG (NDCG) preferred over raw DCG when comparing results across multiple queries?

options.text: NDCG applies a steeper position discount than DCG, making it more sensitive to early retrieval errors
NDCG normalizes by the ideal DCG so all queries map to [0, 1]
Raw DCG rewards retrieving more documents, while NDCG penalizes systems that return more than `k` results
NDCG uses binary relevance labels, which are easier to collect than the graded labels required by raw DCG

questions.explanationMd: Raw DCG values are not directly comparable across queries because queries can have very different gain distributions. NDCG divides by the **ideal DCG**, which puts scores on a shared **0 to 1 scale**.

questions.questionMd: A new retrieval system retrieves a highly relevant document that was not included in the TREC judgment pool. How does this affect the system's measured evaluation score?

options.text: The document is treated as irrelevant because it was never judged, which unfairly penalises the system
The evaluation is restarted with an expanded pool that includes the new system's results
The document receives the highest relevance grade automatically, rewarding novel retrieval
The document is excluded from the metric computation, leaving the system's score unchanged

questions.explanationMd: Under standard pooled evaluation, **unjudged documents are often treated as nonrelevant**. That can penalize systems that find useful documents outside the original pool, which is a known limitation of pooled test collections.

questions.questionMd: The Ideal DCG (`IDCG@k`) is defined as the `DCG@k` of which ranking?

options.text: The ranking produced by the best-performing system in the evaluation pool
The ranking that maximizes Precision@k regardless of graded relevance
The ranking that places all irrelevant documents first to establish a worst-case baseline
The theoretically perfect ranking, where all documents are sorted by descending relevance grade

questions.explanationMd: IDCG is the DCG of the **best possible ordering** for that query under the available relevance labels. It is obtained by sorting documents by **descending relevance grade**.

questions.questionMd: A standard IR test collection consists of exactly three required components. Which of the following correctly identifies all three?

options.text: A ranked retrieval model, a query parser, and a relevance feedback module
A fixed corpus, standardized topics, and human relevance judgments (qrels)
A training set, a validation set, and a held-out test set
An inverted index, a stemmer, and a stop-word list

questions.explanationMd: The classic test collection setup contains **documents**, **topics/queries**, and **relevance judgments (qrels)**. These three pieces allow consistent offline evaluation of retrieval systems.

questions.questionMd: Given the ranked list `R, I, I, R, R` (`R = relevant`, `I = irrelevant`), what is the Average Precision (AP)?
questions.explanationMd: Relevant documents appear at ranks 1, 4, and 5. The precision values there are `1/1 = 1.0`, `2/4 = 0.5`, and `3/5 = 0.6`. Averaging them gives `(1.0 + 0.5 + 0.6)/3 = 0.7`, so **AP = 0.700**.

questions.questionMd: Three queries yield Average Precision scores of `0.72`, `0.45`, and `0.38` for a system. What is the Mean Average Precision (MAP)?

options.text: 0.720, because MAP reports the highest AP score across all queries
0.550, computed as the median of the three AP values
1.550, because MAP sums the AP values without dividing by the number of queries

questions.explanationMd: MAP is simply the **mean of AP over queries**. Here that is `(0.72 + 0.45 + 0.38) / 3 = 0.5166...`, which rounds to **0.517**.

questions.questionMd: In a Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) computation, a query returns a ranked list in which no relevant document appears anywhere in the results. What Reciprocal Rank value is assigned to this query?

options.text: 1.0, because the metric defaults to the maximum when the query cannot be penalised further
The query is excluded from the MRR calculation to avoid distorting the mean
0.0, because no relevant document was found
0.5, as the midpoint value for a partial retrieval failure

questions.explanationMd: Reciprocal Rank is `1/rank_of_first_relevant`. If there is **no relevant result**, there is no such rank, so the contribution is **0.0**.

questions.questionMd: For a single query, the first relevant document appears at rank 3 in the result list. What is the Reciprocal Rank (RR) for this query?
questions.explanationMd: Reciprocal Rank is the inverse of the rank of the first relevant result. If the first relevant result is at rank 3, then `RR = 1/3 ≈ 0.33`.

questions.questionMd: In SMART notation, what does the scheme `lnc` specify for document weighting?

options.text: Natural TF, no IDF, cosine normalization
Log-average TF, natural IDF, no normalization
Logarithmic TF, natural IDF, cosine normalization
Logarithmic TF, no IDF, cosine normalization

questions.explanationMd: In SMART notation, `l` means **logarithmic TF**, `n` means **no IDF**, and `c` means **cosine normalization**. So `lnc` corresponds to **log TF + no IDF + cosine normalization**.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture notes, which traversal strategy is the standard approach in modern IR systems for accumulating TF-IDF scores?

options.text: DAAT (Document At A Time), which processes all terms in a document simultaneously before moving to the next document
TAAT (Term At A Time), which is faster because it avoids the need for a score accumulator
DAAT (Document At A Time), which processes one document at a time and accumulates scores in a dictionary
TAAT (Term At A Time), which processes one term across all matching documents before moving to the next

questions.explanationMd: Modern systems often accumulate candidate scores using a **Document-At-A-Time (DAAT)** traversal. The key idea is to walk postings in a coordinated way and maintain score accumulators for the candidate documents being processed.
